answer_md:
# Async human-in-the-loop hand-off patterns (survey)

Scope: single external consumer (one person, or one person relaying into a chat-only agent), low volume, no API/file access on the consumer side. Not enterprise message-bus territory.

## 1. Shared doc/sheet as a poor-man's queue (Airtable / Notion / Google Sheet)

- **(a) Notification**: two variants exist in the wild, and they matter a lot:
  - *Manual-poll variant* (most common for truly single-consumer, informal setups): there is no notification at all — the consumer is trained to "check the sheet" on some cadence (start of day, before lunch, etc). This is the literal zero-infrastructure baseline and is exactly what indie/solo builders default to before they've been burned by a missed row.
  - *Automation-assisted variant*: Airtable/Notion Automations (or a Zapier/Make trigger watching the base) fire on "new record" / "status = pending" and push an email or Slack DM. This turns the sheet from silent into actively notifying, at the cost of one automation rule.
- **(b) Return**: the consumer edits the same row/page in place (fills a "Result" column, flips "Status" to Done) — no separate return channel needed; the requester's own polling/automation on that same sheet picks up the change.
- **(c) Minimal infra**: one spreadsheet or base. Nothing else if manual-poll; +1 automation/webhook rule if notification-assisted. Free tier of Google Sheets/Notion is enough for one consumer.
- Sources: general practitioner consensus on Airtable/Notion/Sheets as lightweight team tools — https://sync2sheets.com/blog/notion-vs-airtable/ , https://zapier.com/blog/airtable-vs-notion/ (no single canonical "queue" doc exists for this pattern — it's folklore, not a documented product feature; flagged as such below).

## 2. GitHub Issue as the queue (label-driven)

- **(a) Notification**: native GitHub notifications — opening/labeling/assigning an issue triggers GitHub's own notification system (web bell, email digest, mobile push) to anyone watching the repo or assigned to the issue. No custom code required; this is a documented, first-class GitHub feature, not a hack. A GitHub Action can additionally re-notify on specific labels (e.g. "needs-human") via marketplace actions like Issue Label Notifier.
- **(b) Return**: the consumer comments on the issue and/or closes it; the requester (or a workflow_dispatch/issue-comment-triggered Action) picks the comment back up.
- **(c) Minimal infra**: just a git repo (which dark-factory already has) — zero external server, zero paid tier for public repos. Private repos need GitHub Free/Pro/Team to be fine for *public* repos only — required reviewers on *private* repos need Pro/Team, but plain issues+labels+notifications work on any plan/repo visibility.
- Sources: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/events-that-trigger-workflows , https://github.com/marketplace/actions/issue-label-notifier , https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/171932

## 3. GitHub Actions `workflow_dispatch` + Environment protection rules (required reviewers)

- **(a) Notification**: when a job targets a protected environment, the run pauses and GitHub notifies the configured required reviewers through its native notification channels (documented behavior per GitHub Docs on deployments/environments; the exact channel list — email + web + mobile push — is corroborated by practitioner write-ups, not spelled out verbatim in GitHub's own docs, so treat that specific detail as medium-confidence).
- **(b) Return**: reviewer clicks Approve/Reject in the GitHub UI (or via REST API); the workflow run resumes or is cancelled. There's no free-text "result" payload here — it's a binary gate, not a Q&A channel.
- **(c) Minimal infra**: a GitHub repo + an Actions workflow file + an Environment configured in repo settings with 1–6 required reviewers. No external server. Free for public repos on all plans; private repos need Pro/Team for this feature.
- **Fit caveat**: this pattern is a *gate* (proceed / don't), not a *task hand-off with an answer*. It's the wrong shape for "send a research question, get prose back" — good for approvals, poor for the dark-factory use case.
- Sources: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/deploy/configure-and-manage-deployments/control-deployments , https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/deployments-and-environments , https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-01-25-github-actions-environment-protection-rules/view (practitioner corroboration of notification channels)

## 4. Zapier / Make.com "Human in the Loop" (Request Approval / Collect Data)

- **(a) Notification**: built-in, first-class feature (Zapier shipped this as "Human in the Loop" in 2026). Notifies via email, Slack DM/channel, or by triggering a secondary Zap that can fan out to any other connected app.
- **(b) Return**: reviewer opens a Zapier-hosted approval link, clicks Approve/Decline (optionally edits the submitted content or adds a note); the decision and any edited fields flow back into the paused Zap as new fields and the workflow continues.
- **(c) Minimal infra**: zero custom code — but it is a **paid, premium feature** (Professional/Team/Enterprise, not on Free), and every reviewer must have a Zapier account (Professional plans can only route approvals to yourself). So "minimal infra" here means "minimal code, non-zero cost + vendor account requirement."
- Sources: https://help.zapier.com/hc/en-us/articles/38731463206029-Request-approval-to-keep-your-workflow-running-with-Human-in-the-Loop , https://help.zapier.com/hc/en-us/articles/38733184458765-Use-Human-in-the-Loop-to-pause-Zaps-pending-human-review

## 5. Slack slash command + `response_url` (async delayed response)

- **(a) Notification**: the requester's slash command immediately POSTs to your Request URL; Slack requires an HTTP 200 ack within 3 seconds. The actual "someone is notified" moment is whoever is watching the Slack channel where the command was invoked (or a DM you post) — Slack itself doesn't proactively alert a human beyond normal channel/message notifications.
- **(b) Return**: your backend does the (possibly slow) work, then POSTs the result to the `response_url` supplied in the original payload — good for up to ~30 minutes after the command, per Slack's docs, letting you "keep the workflow alive" well past the 3-second ack window.
- **(c) Minimal infra**: you need a small HTTPS endpoint (server or serverless function) to receive the command payload and later push to `response_url`. This is more infrastructure than a webhook-only approach because it requires code you host, plus a Slack app registration.
- Sources: https://docs.slack.dev/interactivity/implementing-slash-commands

## 6. Discord/Slack incoming webhook (one-way notify only)

- **(a) Notification**: an incoming webhook is just a URL you `HTTP POST` to — no bot process, no OAuth, no persistent connection. This is the cheapest possible "ping a human" mechanism that exists among all surveyed patterns.
- **(b) Return**: **none, natively** — incoming webhooks are one-directional (system → human). To get a result back you either (i) have the human reply in the channel/thread and someone/something reads it manually, or (ii) upgrade to a full bot with Gateway reaction/message events (adds a persistent bot process) or an Interactions HTTP endpoint (adds a small server, same shape as the Slack slash-command pattern above).
- **(c) Minimal infra**: one webhook URL + a one-line curl/script wherever the trigger condition is detected (e.g. a cron job or file-watcher). Genuinely the smallest footprint surveyed for the *notify* half of the problem; the *return* half still needs something else layered on (a doc/sheet, a GitHub issue, or a full bot).
- Sources: https://docs.discord.com/developers/platform/webhooks , https://hookdeck.com/webhooks/platforms/guide-to-discord-webhooks-features-and-best-practices

## 7. Email + reply parsing (IMAP/webhook, e.g. n8n "Wait" node)

- **(a) Notification**: an outbound email is sent when the task is created (SMTP or a transactional-email API).
- **(b) Return**: the human replies to the email; a webhook-based inbound-email provider (or IMAP poll) receives the reply, and workflow tooling (n8n's Wait node, or bespoke code) resumes the paused flow, ideally matching the reply to the original request via thread-id/message-id.
- **(c) Minimal infra**: heaviest of the surveyed patterns for a single consumer — needs either an inbound-email-capable provider/webhook receiver, or IMAP polling code, plus reply-threading/dedup logic. Well-documented as a pattern (n8n ships a template for it) but disproportionate for one person, one channel.
- Sources: https://n8n.io/workflows/2907-a-very-simple-human-in-the-loop-email-response-system-using-ai-and-imap/ , https://community.n8n.io/t/wait-node-vs-human-in-the-loop-node/259594

---

## Minimalism ranking (least infra first, for ONE consumer, low volume)

1. **Shared doc/sheet, manual poll** — zero infra beyond the doc itself. Zero notification though (this is dark-factory's current state and its known failure mode: things sit unnoticed).
2. **GitHub Issue + native notifications** — zero external infra (repo already exists), and unlike #1 it gets *free, automatic* notification (GitHub's own system) plus free-text return via comments. Best infra-to-notification ratio surveyed.
3. **One-way webhook ping (Discord/Slack incoming webhook) bolted onto #1 or #2** — one URL + a tiny watcher/cron script; turns a silent queue into a notifying one for near-zero added infra. Still needs a return channel from something else (doc/issue/channel reply read manually).
4. **Zapier/Make Human-in-the-Loop** — zero code, but real recurring cost + reviewer must hold a vendor account.
5. **Slack slash command + response_url** — needs a hosted endpoint (server/serverless) + Slack app registration; more moving parts than 1–4 but gives a clean bidirectional round trip in one system.
6. **GitHub Actions `workflow_dispatch` + environment reviewers** — native and free (public repos), but shaped for binary approve/reject gates, not Q&A payloads; awkward fit if the task needs a prose answer back.
7. **Email + reply parsing** — most moving parts (inbound-email infra, threading, dedup); only worth it if email is already the human's primary interface.

## Relevance to dark-factory

Dark-factory's actual situation — a file-based ticket queue (`engine/store/queue/`), a human relay to one external, non-API, chat-only friend-agent, notify=nothing today (tickets went unnoticed for 2 days), return-leg=manual paste into `results/<ticket>.json` — is almost exactly pattern #1 (shared-doc/manual-poll) already, just with a filesystem directory standing in for the shared sheet. The return leg is already fine (manual paste is a legitimate, documented-common pattern — it's what every one of the surveyed "human types the result back" mechanisms ultimately reduces to for a single non-API consumer). The one broken piece is entirely the notify leg, and pattern #3 (one-way webhook, or its local equivalent) is the cheapest fix that matches dark-factory's actual gap: it adds *notification* without touching the return leg, without spinning up a bot, and without a vendor subscription.

**Concrete cheapest improvement**: don't build a bot, a Slack app, or a Zapier subscription. Add a tiny file-watcher (a `launchd`/cron job or an `fswatch` loop — this repo already uses launchd for the Mochaccino server, so the mechanism is proven locally) that fires the instant a new file lands in `engine/store/queue/`, and have it do the single cheapest possible notify action available: a local macOS notification (`osascript -e 'display notification'` / `terminal-notifier`) and/or a one-line `curl` POST to a Discord or Slack incoming webhook. That is strictly less infrastructure than every bot/Zapier/email pattern surveyed, and it directly fixes the observed failure (tickets sitting silently) without changing anything about how results come back.
notes: The 'shared doc, manual poll' pattern (#1) is folklore/practitioner consensus, not a single documented product feature -- there's no canonical spec for it because it's the absence of automation, not a tool. GitHub's exact reviewer-notification channel list (email + web + mobile push) is corroborated by a practitioner blog (oneuptime), not spelled out verbatim in GitHub's own docs fetched directly -- flagged medium-confidence in the writeup. All other claims (Zapier HITL plan gating, Slack response_url 3s/30min windows, Discord webhook one-way nature, GitHub Actions environment reviewer mechanics) were confirmed against official first-party docs. No code changes or file moves were made outside writing this results file; the ticket file itself was not modified.
